query: effectiveness of mass drug administration for scabies

retrieved_docs.title: Scabies -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Describe the transmission of scabies. Explain how to make the diagnosis of scabies. Summarize strategies for preventing recurrence of scabies. Identify the importance of collaboration and communication among the interprofessional team members in assuring treatment of the entire family of a patient infected with scabies, which will prevent transmission and enhance outcomes for patients with this infection. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Scabies -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Scabies is a significant problem in public health, and one common reason for treatment failure is non-adherence to the treatment regimen. Additionally, improvements in living conditions, avoidance of overcrowding, and avoidance of sharing personal care products can decrease the likelihood of persistence of this pest. While some individuals do obtain relief from symptoms, recurrences are frequent. The level of interprofessional collaboration outlined above is critical to treatment success and optimal outcomes. [Level 5]

retrieved_docs.title: Scabies -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Scabies spreads quickly from person to person via regular skin contact or transmission via clothing or bed sheets. Management involves promptly treating infected persons and their close contacts and decontaminating bedding, towels, and clothing.

retrieved_docs.title: Scabies -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Oral ivermectin is another option, although the United States Food and Drug Administration has not approved its use for scabies treatment. It is administered to individuals ten years and older and given one time. An additional dose is given two weeks later if symptoms persist. Two doses of ivermectin are scabistatic; the second treatment kills mites that have hatched since the first treatment. Oral ivermectin is recommended due to convenience, ease of administration, favorable side effect profile, and safety. Rates of compliance are higher with this treatment modality than with topical permethrin, and the tablet form of ivermectin reduces the likelihood of misuse or inadequate application, as may occur with the topical permethrin [6] . Systemic ivermectin is superior to topical permethrin when treating scabies outbreaks. Ensuring adequate treatment is especially pertinent to the treatment of individuals living in close proximity, such as in homeless shelters, prisons, and healthcare facilities [7] .

retrieved_docs.title: Scabies -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Other options are topical lindane, 5% precipitated sulfur, malathion, and topical ivermectin. Treatment choices may be limited in those with S. scabiei resistance or with limitations due to cost, availability, or potential toxicity, especially among pregnant women and children.

retrieved_docs.title: Scabies -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Treatment failure is common, and isolating the cause can help prevent further infection and limit outbreaks in communities.

retrieved_docs.title: Scabies -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Education about scabies and its presentations is essential for all providers coordinating care, including primary care, emergency medicine, urgent care, pharmacy and even dermatology [6] . Pharmacists are aware of topical creams for scabies treatment and must be able to compound with other ingredients to effectively manage the infection and pruritis. Medication compliance should be encouraged because in most cases, failures are due to non-adherence to the treatment regimen. Nurses will likely be on the front-line for monitoring treatment adherence, providing further patient and family education, and determining other people who will need treatment. Finally, the nurse practitioner should educate the patient on how to eradicate scabies in the home; this is vital to ending the cycle of infestation.

retrieved_docs.title: Scabies -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Scabies is a contagious skin condition caused by the mite Sarcoptes scabiei which burrows into the skin and causes severe itching. Scabies is transmitted by direct skin-to-skin contact or indirectly by contact with contaminated material (fomites). This condition is often challenging to diagnose as many patients may have only subtle symptoms. However, other patients may present with the classic history of exposure, severe pruritis that is worse at night, and reference to other individuals with similar symptoms. This activity reviews the evaluation and treatment of scabies and highlights the role of the interprofessional team in managing patients with this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Scabies -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Regardless of symptoms, all close contacts must receive treatment to prevent reinfection. Many states have legalized Expedited Partner Therapy, which allows physicians to write prescriptions for close contacts of a patient with scabies. This law applies if scabies is considered a sexually transmitted infection in that state. Patients and close contacts should also decontaminate beddings, towels, and clothing at the time that treatment is received [6] .

retrieved_docs.title: Scabies -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Isolation becomes crucial in crowded settings like hospitals, to stop the spread of infection. The bedding, towels, and clothing of the infected individual must be machine-washed in hot water (at least 75 degrees Celcius) and dried with hot air. Topical medication may be given to close contacts for prophylactic therapy [3] [5] .

retrieved_docs.title: Permethrin -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the appropriate indications for permethrin therapy in treating scabies and pediculosis. Assess the response to permethrin treatment and evaluate the resolution of scabies or pediculosis symptoms. Select the appropriate formulation and concentration of permethrin based on patient characteristics, severity of infestation, and treatment goals. Develop communication with patients about the benefits, potential adverse effects, and proper use of permethrin, ensuring their understanding and compliance. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Scabies -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: There are various treatments available for scabies. Evidence shows that when medications are used as directed, the efficacy of standard treatment options is comparable. These include topical permethrin, topical crotamiton, and systemic ivermectin. Adverse reactions are rare to these medications [7] .

retrieved_docs.title: Scabies -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Treatment failure / recurrence  is common, and isolating the cause can help prevent further infection and limit outbreaks in communities. Reasons for treatment failure include not treating close contacts simultaneously, not decontaminating beddings and clothes at the time of treatment, and nonadherence to the treatment regimen.  Treatment failure of crusted scabies can result from ivermectin-resistant Sarcoptes mites. Moxidectin is the recommended therapy for known ivermectin-resistance [4] .

retrieved_docs.title: Antiparasitic Drugs -- Indications -- Ectoparasiticides
retrieved_docs.content: Scabies is a highly contagious pruritic disease caused by Sarcoptes scabiei. Management of scabies is achieved using lindane, permethrin, benzyl benzoate (discontinued in USA), or ivermectin. Resistance to lindane and permethrin have increased over the years, and combination permethrin and oral ivermectin, topical ivermectin, and synergized pyrethrins have led to the highest cure rates. [8] Pediculicides:

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2639
retrieved_docs.content: Sulfur containing product ectoparasiticides are classified as a type of ectoparasiticides, a group that also includes scabicides. As sulfur‑based agents, these ectoparasiticides are employed to treat infestations caused by external parasites such as mites, lice, and scabies‑causing organisms, leveraging sulfur’s antiparasitic properties to eliminate or control the offending organisms and alleviate associated skin irritation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 553
retrieved_docs.content: ANTIPARASITIC PRODUCTS, INSECTICIDES AND REPELLENTS is the broad category that includes the subgroup ECTOPARASITICIDES, INCL. SCABICIDES, INSECTICIDES AND REPELLENTS. Within this subgroup, the class ectoparasiticides, incl. scabicides is contained, and Sulfur containing product ectoparasiticides represent a specific type of ectoparasiticides, incl. scabicides. This hierarchical organization clarifies how the general anti‑parasitic product category is subdivided into increasingly specific ectoparasiticidal agents, with sulfur‑based formulations constituting a particular subclass, aiding in classification, regulation, and appropriate selection of treatments for ectoparasite control.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2638
retrieved_docs.content: ANTIPARASITIC PRODUCTS, INSECTICIDES AND REPELLENTS is a broad therapeutic class that encompasses ECTOPARASITICIDES, INCL. SCABICIDES, INSECTICIDES AND REPELLENTS; the latter represent a specific subgroup of ant parasitic agents that target ectoparasites and include scabicides, insecticides, and repellents.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 2434
retrieved_docs.content: Other ectoparasiticides, including scabicides in ATC, are classified as a subset of ectoparasiticides, including scabicides.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 1435
retrieved_docs.content: ANTIPARASITIC PRODUCTS, INSECTICIDES AND REPELLENTS is the broad category that includes ECTOPARASITICIDES, INCL. SCABICIDES, INSECTICIDES AND REPELLENTS as a more specific subclass. Within that subclass, ectoparasiticides, incl. scabicides represent an even narrower group of ectoparasitic agents. Pyrethrines, incl. synthetic compounds, ectoparasiticides, incl. scabicides are a specific type of synthetic ectoparasiticide that falls under the ectoparasiticides, incl. scabicides group. This hierarchical classification clarifies how these products are organized from general antiparasitic and insecticidal formulations down to particular synthetic scabicidal agents, aiding in their regulation, therapeutic use, and safety assessment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 1 -- Community 125
retrieved_docs.content: taxonomy links anti‑infective and antiparasitic agents—including antihelmintics, antiprotozoáls, antitrematodals, ectoparasiticides, scabicides, and related pesticides—to their constituent compounds, therapeutic subclasses, formulation details, safety profiles, and interaction warnings, highlighting the interconnected nature of antiparasitic, antimalarial, antischistosomal, trypanocidal, and antifungal pharmacology and the potential for dual‑use drugs in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 1545
retrieved_docs.content: ANTIPARASITIC PRODUCTS, INSECTICIDES AND REPELLENTS is a broad therapeutic category that encompasses ECTOPARASITICIDES, INCL. SCABICIDES, INSECTICIDES AND REPELLENTS, indicating that the latter is a specific subclass of the former. Within this subclass, ectoparasiticides, incl. scabicides represents an even more narrowly defined group, making it a further subset of ECTOPARASITICIDES, INCL. SCABICIDES, INSECTICIDES AND REPELLENTS. This hierarchical organization clarifies that the most general class—ANTIPARASITIC PRODUCTS, INSECTICIDES AND REPELLENTS—covers a wide range of chemical agents used to control ectoparasites, scabies, and insects, while the subsequent levels identify progressively more specific types of these agents, aiding clinicians and pharmacists in selecting appropriate treatments for parasitic and insect‑related conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 1400
retrieved_docs.content: ANTIPARASITIC PRODUCTS, INSECTICIDES AND REPELLENTS represents a broad therapeutic category that includes the more specific group ECTOPARASITICIDES, INCL. SCABICIDES, INSECTICIDES AND REPELLENTS. This latter group is itself a subclass of the broader class ectoparasiticides, incl. scabicides. Within the ectoparasiticides, incl. scabicides category, the ATC‑defined subgroup Other ectoparasiticides, incl. scabicides in ATC is a distinct subclass. Together, these entities form a hierarchical classification in which anti‑parasitic products and repellents encompass ectoparasiticides that contain scabicides, insecticides and repellents, and the ATC “Other ectoparasiticides, incl. scabicides” are a specific subset of that broader ectoparasiticide class.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 1 -- Community 114
retrieved_docs.content: category. Ectoparasiticides comprise scabicides, insecticides and repellents. Dimethicone is categorized as “Other ectoparasiticide, incl. scabicides” and contains pyrantel‑pamoate as an inactive ingredient, illustrating a formulation that combines a silicone‑based ectoparasiticide with an anthelmintic. The subclass “Pyrethrines, incl. synthetic compounds, ectoparasiticides, incl. scabicides” includes phenothrin, which is also listed as a pesticide via an inverse‑ISA relationship. Sulfur‑containing ectoparasiticides constitute an additional chemical subclass. The broader group “Drugs, medicaments and biological substances causing adverse effects in therapeutic use” lists anti‑helminthic agents as a distinct adverse‑effect‑causing class alongside thrombolytics and systemic antibiotics. A clinically significant drug interaction is highlighted: dihydroergotamine is contraindicated when co‑administered with the antifungal posaconazole, which is also classified as a trypanocidal agent

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 207
retrieved_docs.content: sulfur‑containing ectoparasiticides, are likewise classified under ectoparasiticides, incl. scabicides and are used to control ectoparasites such as scabies. Phenothrin is a specific compound within the synthetic pyrethrin class and functions as a pesticide for ectoparasite control. Dimethicone formulations may contain pyrantel pamoate (144 mg/mL oral suspension) as an inactive ingredient, demonstrating that silicone‑based ectoparasiticide products can incorporate additional antiparasitic agents.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 1 -- Community 120
retrieved_docs.content: its inclusion in standardized treatment protocols, and its relevance to conditions such as scabies, while also situating related immunomodulatory agents like crizanlizumab and antibacterial agents like gentamicin within the same therapeutic landscape.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 555
retrieved_docs.content: Phenothrin is a synthetic pyrethroid that belongs to the broader class of pyrethrines, a group of synthetic compounds commonly employed as ectoparasiticides and scabicides. As a member of the pyrethrines, phenothrin functions as a pesticide, targeting external parasites such as lice, mites, and the organisms that cause scabies. This classification highlights phenothrin’s role within the pyrethrine family of ectoparasiticides and underscores its utility in pest control and the treatment of parasitic skin conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 2 -- Community 938
retrieved_docs.content: ANTIPARASITIC PRODUCTS, INSECTICIDES AND REPELLENTS represent a broad category of chemical agents that encompass ECTOPARASITICIDES, INCL. SCABICIDES, INSECTICIDES AND REPELLENTS as a more specific subgroup. Within this subgroup, the class ectoparasiticides, incl. scabicides is defined, and several distinct types of ectoparasiticides are identified. Pyrethrines, incl. synthetic compounds, ectoparasiticides, incl. scabicides are classified as a type of ectoparasiticide, and the specific compound phenothrin is a member of this pyrethrine class. Phenothrin is also recognized as a pesticide. In addition, Sulfur containing product ectoparasiticides are another subclass of ectoparasiticides, incl. scabicides. Together, these relationships illustrate a hierarchical organization in which broad antiparasitic and insecticidal products contain increasingly specific ectoparasiticidal agents, including synthetic pyrethrines such as phenothrin and sulfur‑based formulations, all of which are considered pesticides.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 1146
retrieved_docs.content: ANTIPARASITIC PRODUCTS, INSECTICIDES AND REPELLENTS is a broad therapeutic class that includes ECTOPARASITICIDES, INCL. SCABICIDES, INSECTICIDES AND REPELLENTS as a more specific subgroup. Within this subgroup, the category ectoparasiticides, incl. scabicides is defined, and it further comprises distinct subclasses such as Other ectoparasiticides, incl. scabicides in ATC and Sulfur containing product ectoparasiticides, both of which are classified as ectoparasiticides, incl. scabicides. Other ectoparasiticides, incl. scabicides in ATC specifically lists dimethicone as a member. In formulations where dimethicone is used, pyrantel pamoate 144 MG/ML Oral Suspension is identified as an inactive ingredient. This hierarchical organization clarifies how various ectoparasiticidal agents and related products are categorized within the larger ant parasitic, insecticidal, and repellent pharmacological framework, supporting clinicians and pharmacists in product selection and understanding of component relationships.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 207
retrieved_docs.content: for alcohol‑dependence treatment, illustrating cross‑application of psychotropic agents in addiction care. Drugs used in nicotine dependence are identified as a subclass of addictive‑disorder agents and are associated with the agent vareniklin, reflecting their role in nicotine‑cessation strategies. In the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) system, ANTIPARASITIC PRODUCTS, INSECTICIDES AND REPELLENTS is the overarching category that encompasses ectoparasiticides, scabicides, insecticides and repellents. The more specific subclass ECTOPARASITICIDES, INCL. SCABICIDES, INSECTICIDES AND REPELLENTS is an inverse‑ISA of this group. Dimethicone is listed as a member of this ectoparasiticide subclass. Other ectoparasiticides, including scabicides, pyrethrines (synthetic ectoparasiticides) and sulfur‑containing ectoparasiticides, are likewise classified under ectoparasiticides, incl. scabicides and are used to control ectoparasites such as scabies. retrieved_docs.content: agents, and various traditional antiseptic classes are organized by their chemical nature, therapeutic category (antiprotozoal, antibacterial, antiseptic, disinfectant), and specific clinical applications such as treatment of leishmaniasis, trypanosomiasis, systemic bacterial infections, and incorporation into topical formulations. Understanding these interconnections aids clinicians and formulators in selecting appropriate agents for infection control, systemic therapy, and product development.
